coredata.dc:title: Integrating optimal amount of carbon dots in g-C3N4 for enhanced visible light photocatalytic H2 evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Jinnan
Yang, Xiaohong
Fu, Haitao
Ran, Xiaoli
Zhao, Chen
An, Xizhong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) hold great promise for photocatalytic application owning to their low production cost, unique optical properties, as well as excellent stability and conductivity. Integrating CDs in graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets helps to broaden visible light absorption, retard charge recombination and promote photoelectrons transport. Herein, we demonstrated a simple strategy to introduce CDs on g-C3N4 nanosheets by hydrothermal treatment of ginkgo leaves followed by thermal polymerization of urea. We found that there was were two volcano-trends in the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate with the increase of CDs loading. As a result, the optimized CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites demonstrated a superior hydrogen evolution rate as high as 3.12 mmol g−1·h−1 and 14.4% apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was achieved at 420 nm visible light irradiation. The contribution of CDs towards the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution enhancement was discussed in depth via experiment characterization and Density functional theory (DFT) calculation. This work may shed light on the rational design and bottom-up synthesis of eco-friendly energy conversion materials with high-performance and low cost.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst H2 evolution
Visible-light-driven
Carbon dots
G-C3N4

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51974086 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University ( N2215027 , N2225028 and N2124007-1 ). The authors would like to appreciate Mr. Jilong Gao from Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the XPS analysis. Special thanks for the instrumental or data analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University and Beijing Perfectlight Technology CO, LTD.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scheme on the preparation of the CDs/g-C3N4 composites, (b) TEM image of the pristine g-C3N4, (c) TEM image of the 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4, (d) XRD pattern and (e) FTIR pattern of the pristine g-C3N4, 0.005% CDs/g-C3N4 and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4, respectively.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) Band gap energies, (c) VB XPS spectra, and (d) Mott-Schottky plots of the pristine g-C3N4, 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4, respectively.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) C and N content, (c) C1s spectrum and (d) N1s spectrum of the pristine g-C3N4, 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4, respectively.

(a) H2 production rate and (b) H2 production volume in 4 h under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) of different CDs content in CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalysts, (c) AQE for different mass of 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 in the water splitting reactor, (d) Typical cycling testing of H2 production under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) catalyzed by the pristine g-C3N4 (the black one) and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 (the red one). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Wavelength-dependent AQE (red part) of H2 evolution towards 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 and the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra (black part). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) The transient photocurrent response, (b) Electrochemical impedance spectra, (c) PL spectra at an excitation of 340 nm and (d) Time-resolved transient PL decay.
Optimized unit cells, calculated band structure and density of state (DOS) for g-C3N4 (a–c) and for CDs/g-C3N4 (d–f).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) Tauc plots, (c) Mott Schottky plots and (d) Band structure of alignments of the pristine g-C3N4, CDs/g-C3N4 and Pt/CDs/g-C3N4, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the charge transfer of CDs/g-C3N4 with 3 wt% Pt loading for photocatalytic activity induced by light irradiation and photocatalytic mechanism.
Calculations on various CDs decorated on g-C3N4.

head.ce:title: Integrating optimal amount of carbon dots in g-C3N4 for enhanced visible light photocatalytic H2 evolution

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Ecological Metallurgy of Multimetallic Mineral

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) hold great promise for photocatalytic application owning to their low production cost, unique optical properties, as well as excellent stability and conductivity. Integrating CDs in graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets helps to broaden visible light absorption, retard charge recombination and promote photoelectrons transport. Herein, we demonstrated a simple strategy to introduce CDs on g-C3N4 nanosheets by hydrothermal treatment of ginkgo leaves followed by thermal polymerization of urea. We found that there was were two volcano-trends in the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate with the increase of CDs loading. As a result, the optimized CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites demonstrated a superior hydrogen evolution rate as high as 3.12 mmol g−1·h−1 and 14.4% apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was achieved at 420 nm visible light irradiation. The contribution of CDs towards the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution enhancement was discussed in depth via experiment characterization and Density functional theory (DFT) calculation. This work may shed light on the rational design and bottom-up synthesis of eco-friendly energy conversion materials with high-performance and low cost.

CDs modified g-C3N4 hybrid was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of ginkgo leaves followed by thermal polymerization of urea. The H2 evolution rate of g-C3N4 improved 4.81 times after modified with CDs due to fast charge separation and the increase of surface-active sites.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Solar light is the most abundant renewable resource on the surface of the earth, and hydrogen has been regarded as one of the most promising energy carriers of next-generation. Smartly converting intermittent solar energy into storable and transportable hydrogen energy via photocatalytic water splitting has drawn tremendous intension to meet the ongoing energy crisis and environmental pollution [1–3]. And exploring efficient and scalable photocatalysts is the prerequisite for this purpose. In the past decades, numerous semiconductor photocatalysts have been reported, including inorganic metal oxides/sulfides [4,5], transition metal layered double hydroxides [6,7], perovskite oxides [8,9], MOF compounds and polymer semiconductors [10,11], etc. Among them, g-C3N4 an organic semiconductor, is an attractive candidate due to its suitable band structure, excellent chemical stability, harmless to the environment and low production cost [12]. But it is also limited by some drawbacks, such as insufficient solar light absorption, high photo-excited charge recombination and poor conductivity [13,14].

Up to now, a large number of studies have been reported to improve the photocatalytic activity of pristine g-C3N4 by means of morphology control [15], modification of g-C3N4 with heteroatoms [16], secondary semiconductor [17], vacancy defects [18], and carbonaceous materials [19]. As a zero-dimensional material, CDs exhibit unique photo-induced charge transfer and reservoir properties, as well as excellent electric conductivity [20,21]. It is generally believed that decorating g-C3N4 with tiny amount of CDs helps to broaden its photo absorption region, retard the charge recombination and increase the number of active reaction sites [22,23]. For example, Liu et al. [24] synthesized CDs implanted g-C3N4 nanosheets exhibited the optimal H2 evolution of 88.1 μmol⋅g−1⋅h−1 for photocatalytic overall water splitting. Similarly, Li et al. [11] prepared g-C3N4 coupled with CDs to obtain an impressive H2 production rate of 2.321 mmol⋅g−1⋅h−1. However, the improvement of the activity is limited. Moreover, the in-depth explanation about the influence of CDs on light absorption and charge carriers is necessary. At the same time, incorporation with too many CDs may inevitably affect the backbone of g-C3N4 and block the light absorption, resulting in a significant drop of photocatalytic activity [25]. Thus, the right amount of CDs is also crucial to achieve high-performance of CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalysts.

Herein, we developed a simple strategy to introduce the different contents of CDs in g-C3N4 nanosheets by hydrothermal treatment of ginkgo leaves followed by thermal polymerization of urea. The role of CDs in the process of photocatalytic H2 evolution towards CDs/g-C3N4 was studied qualitatively and quantitatively via experiment and simulation studies. We found that there was two volcano-trends in the H2 evolution rate with the increase of CDs loading amount. And the optimized CDs/g-C3N4 exhibited the highest H2 evolution rate (3120 μmol⋅g−1⋅h−1) as far as we know (Table 1 ). In comparison with pristine g-C3N4, incorporation with CDs helps to broaden its light absorption region, accelerate the photo-excited charge transfer and provide more reaction active sites.

CDs were synthesized by a typical hydrothermal method. The fallen ginkgo leaves on the campus were collected and washed several times by ultrapure water, then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h 5 g of the dry ginkgo leaves were weighted and smashed, then dispersed into 15 mL ultrapure water. The mixture was transferred into a 30 mL Teflon stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After being naturally cooled to room temperature, the brownish-yellow suspension was collected by suction filtration. Finally, the as-prepared CDs were collected via the continuous rotary evaporation of the brownish-yellow suspension. The resultant CDs were weighted and dispersed into ultrapure water to form a 2 mg/L CDs suspension for further use.

To synthesize the g-C3N4 nanosheets, typically, 10 g urea powder was placed into a covered alumina crucible and then heated to 550 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min in a muffle furnace and maintained for 3 h. The as-produced g-C3N4 (roughly 0.2 g bright yellow powder) was obtained afterwards. For the preparation of CDs/g-C3N4, 10 g urea powder and several drops of 2 mg/L CDs solution were added to 10 mL ultrapure water with continuous stirring for 0.5 h, with the weight ratio of CDs: g-C3N4 spanning from 0.0025% to 10%. Then the mixture was put into a covered alumina crucible. The calcination treatment was the same as that of preparing pristine g-C3N4. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the products were collected and ground into powders. The colour of the products gradually changed from light yellow to brown with the increase of mass ratio. The obtained samples were g-C3N4 decorated with CDs with CDs/g-C3N4 weight ratios ranging from 0.0025% to 10%.

Crystal structure and phase composition of the as-prepared samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using the Phillips X ′Pert Multipurpose X-ray Diffraction System (MPD) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) in the 2θ ranging from 5° to 90° at a scan rate of 0.13°s−1. The morphologies of as-prepared products were collected by Zeiss field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM). The detailed morphology and structure were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) at an accelerated voltage of 100 kV (FEI tecnaiG20). The optical properties were characterized by ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption measurement (Shimadzu UV-2600). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by a fluorescence lifetime spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific Lumina, US). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was conducted based on a FTIR spectrometer (PerkinElmer Frontier) using KBr pellet as the background. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected by Thermo K-ALPHA using monochromatic Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV). A Kelvin probe apparatus (ThermoFisher ESCALAB 250Xi) with a sensitivity of 1 mV was employed to test the work function value (W) using a gold mesh Kelvin probe (3 mm diameter). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore size distribution of the products were measured from nitrogen physisorption isotherms (adsorption-desorption branches) at 77 K via a Micrometric ASAP 2020 instrument.

The photo-electrochemical testing was conducted by a conventional three-electrode model coupled with an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai, China). In this system, Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt wire electrode were employed as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. A piece of fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass with a fixed area of 3 × 1 cm covered with the as-prepared samples was used as the working electrode. To prepare the working electrode, it is necessary to ensure the loading of the as-prepared samples was approximately equal. Specifically, 3 mg of the g–C3N4–based composite was added into 1 mL ethanol and sonicated for 15 min. The slurry was dripped onto the FTO glass every three drops, and then dried at 60 °C for the next coating layer. This coating process was repeated five times. Subsequently, the coated FTO glass can be used as a working electrode after calcination at 300 °C for 1 h. The three-electrode system was placed in a 100 mL Quartz electrolytic cell with 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution as electrolyte. A 300 W Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300C) was used as the light source to test the photocurrent (I-T curves) with 20 s interval. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was collected at the frequency of 0.01–105 Hz with the Alternating Current (AC) amplitude of 10 mV at the open-circuit voltage. The Mott-Schottky curve (MS) was performed at the frequency of 500, 1000 and 1500 Hz, respectively.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution test was conducted by a Labsolar-6A photocatalytic system (Beijing Perfect-light Technology Co., Ltd.). As for the test, 20 mg photocatalysts were dispersed in 100 mL DI water solution containing 10 vol% TEOA sacrificial agent and 3 wt% Pt co-catalyst. Specifically, 3 wt% Pt co-catalyst was loaded by adding 11 μL of H2PtCl6 (0.3 M) aqueous solution into the suspension followed by the irradiation of a 300 W Xe lamp coupled with a UV cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm). Pt co-catalyst was on the surface of catalyst because of in situ photo-deposited [26,27]. The solution was transferred into an externally illuminated photoreactor connected to a closed circulation system with continuous stirring. It is necessary to load with argon for half an hour to remove residual air before photocatalytic reactions. In the experiment, the temperature of the reactor was kept at 5 °C using the circulating water pump. The light source was a 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300, Perfect Light) equipped with cut-off filters (AM 1.5 G and λ > 420 nm). After entering the circulatory system, the produced H2 was sent to a gas chromatography (GC7900, TCD detector and 5 Å molecular sieve column) for qualitative and quantitative analysis. Calculation details on the AQE of H2 production were provided below Table S2.

The Cambridge Sequential Total Energy Package (CASTEP) module of the Materials Studio (MS) software was used to conduct the DFT calculations, using the plane-wave pseudopotential method. The geometry optimization of the proposed models was performed by generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burkee-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional. The band gap calculated by PBE functional will be lower than the real value because of its own limitations. The k-point meshes were set as 3 × 3 × 1 that automatically generated by using the Monkhorst-Pack method and the cut-off energy was set as 520 eV. To eliminate the interactions of the periodic structures, the vacuum layer was built to be 15 Å. The convergence thresholds for geometry optimization of all models were set as 1.0 × 10−5 eV per atom for energy, 0.001 Å for maximum displacement, 0.03 eV Å−1 for maximum force, and 0.05 GPa for maximum pressure. The self-consistent convergence tolerance was set at 1.0 × 10−5 eV per atom.

CDs possess excellent optical properties, high conductivity and large surface area. Taking advantage of these unique properties, a set of CDs/g-C3N4 composites with different weight ratios were synthesized in this work. The synthetic scheme is presented in Fig. 1 a. The morphology of the pristine g-C3N4 and CDs/g-C3N4 composites were recorded by TEM. The as-prepared g-C3N4 shows the layered nano-structure (Fig. 1b), which is consistent with previous reports [11]. For 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4, it is observed that the CDs (4–5 nm in diameter, marked by red arrows) were successfully loaded on g-C3N4 nanosheets and the CDs distribution was relatively uniform (Fig. 1c). The crystal structure and the composition of CDs/g-C3N4 were analyzed by XRD. The peaks at 13.0°and 27.3° were indexed as the (100) and (002) planes of g-C3N4 (Fig. 1d), which correspond to the repeating unit in-plane of tri-s-triazine and the stacked sandwich of conjugated aromatic structure (Fig. S1a) [28]. It is noted that even though the intrinsic graphite structure of g-C3N4 was retained, the XRD peak intensity was drastically reduced with the increase of CDs loading, suggesting decreased crystallinity of the g-C3N4 after decoration with CDs, which means the existence of CDs hampered the thermal polymerization of sti-s-triazine units and the stacking of g-C3N4 sheets [29]. The functional groups and chemical bond vibrations were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 1e). The sharp peak at 810 cm−1 was originated from the out-of-plane bending mode of the heptazine ring, and the typical peaks at 3000-3500−1 and 1200-1700 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and N–CN heterocycles located in the melon framework (Fig. S1b). No new peaks appeared for the CDs/g-C3N4 composites suggesting that adding a small number of CDs cannot significantly change the original framework of g-C3N4. And the lower vibration frequency indicates that CDs in the composites work as the electron donor, which is beneficial for the charge transfer [30].

The optical properties of the CDs/g-C3N4 composites were studied by UV–vis adsorption spectra. And the band gap energy of samples were estimated by the Tauc plots, which was converted by the UV–vis spectrum to an αhv∧2 vs hv curve based on Kubellka-Munk function [31] (a, h and v refer to the absorption coefficient, Planck constant and the speed of light, respectively). The absorption peak of g-C3N4 was centered at 340 nm (Fig. 2 a), which was correlated with the band gap of approximately 2.31 eV (Fig. 2b). Notably, there was an obvious red shift of the absorption edge in the spectrum of CDs/g-C3N4, particularly, with ascendance of CDs loading. This results in the reduced band gap of 2.04 eV and 1.97 eV for 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4, respectively. The reduction of band gap could be caused by the quantum confinement effect of CDs [32]. The overall light absorption in the range of 200–800 nm was enhanced with the increase of CDs loading amount, which may favour the process of photo excitation and electron-hole generation.

Aside from an appropriate band gap, the proper matching of the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) levels of a photocatalyst is also a prerequisite for H2 evolution. The XPS was used to determine the ionization potential (equivalent to VB energy). It is estimated that the VB of the pristine g-C3N4, 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 were 1.77 eV, 1.70 eV and 1.67 eV, respectively (Fig. 2c). The shift of VB indicated the change of the semiconductor electronic structure [27]. According to the Mott–Schottky plots in Fig. 2d, the positive slope with various frequencies reveals n-type semiconductor characteristics. The flat band potentials (determined by extrapolating to C−2 = 0) of g-C3N4, 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 were −0.84, −0.64 and −0.60 V (vs. Ag/AgCl electrode), respectively. The measured potentials can be converted to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) scale via the Nernst equation as Eq. (1):
(1)
E
f b
(
N H E
)
=
E
f b
( A g / A g C l )
+
0.197

The calculated flat band potentials with reference to the NHE were −0.64, −0.44 and −0.4 V, respectively. Generally, the flat band potential (Efb) can be used to calculate the CB potential (Ec) by the Eq. (2):
(2)
E c
−
E
f b
=
0.1
e V

The Ec of g-C3N4, 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 were −0.54, −0.34 and −0.30 eV, respectively. Thus, the band structure of pristine g-C3N4 and CDs/g-C3N4 composites were determined and depicted in Fig. S2, it is concluded that for both g-C3N4 and the CDs/g-C3N4, their VB and CB could span the redox potential of water splitting towards H2 evolution. The 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 composite exhibits the narrowest bandgap with more positive CB, which facilitates the generation of photo-carriers. On the other hand, more positive CB helps to reduce thermionic emission induced by the high potential barrier height of the semiconductor-Pt contact [33].

XPS was used to verify the element composition, molecular structure, atomic valence, and chemical bonds of the CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Fig. 3 a presents the XPS survey spectra of the pristine g-C3N4, 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites. Two strong peaks of C ls and N ls indicate that CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were only composed of carbon and nitrogen. Moreover, the content of carbon element was calculated based on XPS data. The results displayed in Fig. 3b suggest that the carbon contents gradually increased with the decoration of CDs, and the carbon to nitrogen ratio changed from 3:4 (pristine g-C3N4) to 3:2.55 (1.0% CDs/g-C3N4), which confirmed that the CDs were successfully integrated into the g-C3N4 nanosheets. There are two peaks in the spectrum of the pristine g-C3N4 (284.6 and 288.0 eV) (Fig. 3c), which is attributed to the foreign graphitic carbon (CC) used as calibration reference and sp2 hybrid carbon in the carbon network (NCN) in g-C3N4. The peak of sp2 hybridized carbon in 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 increase slightly with the decoration of CDs. Furthermore, the content of CC increased from 15% (pristine g-C3N4) to 40% (1.0% CDs/g-C3N4) indicating that the interaction between CDs and g-C3N4 caused a change in the chemical state (Fig. S3a). The N ls (Fig. 3d) peaks are fitted into four peaks centered at 398.5, 400.2, 401.3, and 404.4 eV, respectively. The hybrid sp2 aromatic N (C–NC, 398.5 eV) in the triazine unit causes a major signal peak in each sample. The binding energy peaks of 400.2 eV and 401.3 eV are attributed to the bridging nitrogen atom (N-(C)3) and the amino group with hydrogen (NH bond or NH2 bond), respectively. The weakest peak in CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (404.4 eV) is related to the charging effect or positive charge localization of the cyano group and the heterocyclic ring. The chemical state of the N element is not changed significantly, and the content of C–NC is slightly reduced, indicating that the carbon replaced the nitrogen in the heterocyclic ring partly, which is consistent with the increase of CDs content in the above-mentioned CC bond (Fig. S3b).

To evaluate the photocatalytic performance of various g-C3N4 and CDs/g-C3N4 samples, the photocatalytic H2 evolution test was conducted under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) with triethanolamine (TEOA) as the hole acceptor and Pt as a co-catalyst. Fig. 4 a shows the trend of H2 evolution rate with the CDs loading content. Interestingly, the highest H2 productions are commonly discussed with a tiny amount of the CDs loading (0.001%–0.25%) in composites according to the previous reports (as shown in Table 1). However, in our work, we found that there was were two volcano-trends in the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate if continue to increase the content of CDs loading up to 10%. It can be seen in Fig. 4a that there are two peaks of H2 evolution rate at 0.005% CDs/g-C3N4 (1.56 mmol⋅g−1⋅h−1) and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 (3.12 mmol⋅g−1⋅h−1), respectively. Correspondingly, the H2 production in 4 h increased from 3.12 mmol to 12.5 mmol (1.0% CDs/g-C3N4) (Fig. 4b). The H2 evolution performances of CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites under solar irradiation (AM1.5G) were also tested (Fig. S4). It confirms the same regularity as the visible light results, showing that the optimized 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 demonstrated a superior H2 evolution rate as high as 4.81 mmol⋅g−1⋅h−1. Besides, the relationship between the amount of catalyst added in water and the corresponding AQE was analyzed. It is found that AQE increases to a maximum of 14.4% when adding 20 mg 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst at λ = 420 nm (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the H2 evolution rate remained in the 4 cycles of 24 h (4 h per cycle, Fig. 4d) in the photocatalytic stability test, indicating the excellent stabilities of the pristine g-C3N4 nanosheets and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 composites. The XRD (Fig. S5), TEM (Fig. S6) and UV–vis (Fig. S7) of the used samples were also characterized to determine the stability of the photocatalysts. It was found that the morphology and composition did not change much after the hydrogen evolution reaction. The above results indicate that CDs/g-C3N4 is a promising photocatalyst candidate with excellent photocatalytic activity and stability.

To further understand the contribution of CDs in CDs/g-C3N4 samples for the highly enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution, the wavelength-dependent H2 evolution rate towards 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 were carried out to investigate the relationship between light absorption and photocatalytic activity. The AQE at different wavelength (irradiated by a 300 W Xe lamp using a bandpass filter of λ±20 nm for 350, 420, 475, 550 and 650 nm) were approximately calculated, and the calculation details were listed in Table S2. Fig. 5 shown the trend of AQE was not fully coincident with the light absorption spectra of 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4, except the AQE on λ = 420 ± 20 nm. It indicates that the absorption of the photons in the wider band was not the key factor in above improved photocatalytic performance. In other words, the increase of visible light absorption caused by a small amount of CDs does not contribute much to the H2 production enhancement. Based on previous reports, the light absorption by CDs may be dissipated in the form of thermal emission [23,24]. And the superior H2 production rate of 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 in the visible range might be explained by the acceleration of charge transfer and surface reactions. Besides, the specific surface area was dramatically increased (from 82.8 to 212.3 m2/g, Fig. S8) with 1.0% CDs loading, which offers more reactive sites for the redox reaction.

The movement of photo-generated charge carriers is one of the key factors determining the overall efficiency of H2 evolution. In this work, photoelectrochemical and photoluminescence analysis were conducted to study the underlying catalytic mechanism. Fig. 6 a exhibits the reproducible transient photocurrent responses of various samples. The photocurrent density obtained from the 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 composites was calculated to be 2.3 and 3.6 times higher than that of the pristine g-C3N4, suggesting more efficient charge separation occurs in the composites with relatively higher CDs integration (1.0% CDs/g-C3N4). The increase in photocurrent density could also contribute to the improved visible light absorption originated by the CDs loading. To further study the charge transfer process, the EIS were collected and analyzed according to the equivalent circuit. The pristine g-C3N4 nanosheets had a large resistance hindering the migration of photo-generated charge carriers to enter the reaction. In comparison, both 0.005% and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 composites exhibited a much smaller impedance (Fig. 6b), which greatly favors the charge separation of photo generated electron-hole pair, resulting in the acceleration of catalytic reaction kinetics. Besides, the electron-hole recombination rate was characterized by PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 6c, the pristine g-C3N4 nanosheets had a strong emission peak at 470 nm under the excitation of 340 nm, which originated from the inter-band recombination of electrons and holes. Compared with g-C3N4, the PL emission intensity of 0.005% CDs/g-C3N4 was significantly reduced, and 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 was the lowest, indicating that the decoration of CDs can greatly promote the photo-generated electron transfer and further suppress the electron-holes recombination. The time-resolved PL decay spectra were also conducted to measure the lifetimes of charge carriers. A tri-exponential function fitting was employed to analyse the PL decay curves, and the average lifetimes were calculated as shown in Fig. 6d and Table S4. It is worth noting that the average PL lifetime of CDs/g-C3N4 was longer than that of the pristine g-C3N4. The results confirmed the improved photo-excited electrons transport rate of CDs/g-C3N4, which can improve the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution.

To reveal the effect of the CDs on the electronic structure and the band structure in theoretical sights, the DFT calculations were performed by MS commercial software. The models of g-C3N4 and CDs/g-C3N4 were optimized by geometry optimization (Fig. 7 a, d). There were two C sites and three N sites in the structure of g-C3N4, which are named as Cl, C2, Nl, N2, and N3, respectively (Fig. S9). The calculated results of band structures of g-C3N4 and CDs/g-C3N4 are shown in Fig. 7b, e. The density of states (DOS) of g-C3N4 and CDs/g-C3N4 are presented in Fig. 7c and f, respectively. It shows that the bandgap of the pristine g-C3N4 is 1.276 eV, which is smaller than the experimental value because the self-interaction is neglected in the PBE functional [36]. The bandgap of CDs/g-C3N4 decreases to 0.07 eV after introducing the CDs, the trend of reduced band gap demonstrates that the CDs decoration reduced the band gap successfully. The DOS diagram shows that the VB of g-C3N4 and CDs/g-C3N4 is mainly composed of Np orbitals, while the CB is composed of Cp and Np. This is mainly because that the electronegativity of nitrogen is greater than that of carbon.

To understand the interaction between cocatalysts and semiconductors, the UV–vis and Mott Schottky plots of the pristine g-C3N4, CDs/g-C3N4 and Pt/CDs/g-C3N4 were conducted. As shown in Fig. 8 a, there was a tiny redshift in absorption spectra after loading with Pt. Correspondingly, the band gap of the CDs/g-C3N4 was slightly reduced from 1.97 eV to 1.90 eV with the Pt loading (Fig. 8b). Moreover, the flat band potentials slightly changed from −0.60 V to −0.56 V (vs. Ag/AgCl electrode) as shown in Fig. 8c. Consequently, the bandgaps of different samples were presented in Fig. 8d, it is noted that Pt could not only lower the reaction energy barrier of the water splitting reaction, but also lower the position of conduction band. To explain the transfer of the electrons, the Work function (W) of Pt, CDs and g-C3N4 were determined to be 4.48, 3.7 and 2.7 eV, respectively (Fig. S10). The work function of g-C3N4 is smaller than CDs and Pt. Therefore, when CDs and g-C3N4 intimately contact, the electrons may migrate from g-C3N4 to CDs to achieve Fermi level equilibrium between them. With loading of Pt co-catalysts, electrons could be injected into the Pt from the CDs driven by the most negative Ef as shown in Fig. 9 . Finally, the initial equilibrium state of Ef between CDs and g-C3N4 was broken, and a new equilibrium state is established. Thus, the photoexcited charge carrier migration pathway across the Pt/CDs/g-C3N4 interface is evidenced from g-C3N4 through CDs to Pt [37].

Based on the above results, a plausible mechanism on the enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution towards the CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites was proposed in Fig. 9. Once exposed under light illumination, electrons and holes in the g-C3N4 started to be separated with the electrons transition from VB to CB. Normally, the photogenerated charge carriers quickly recombined, leading to the relatively low activity. When CDs were integrated with the g-C3N4, as the work function of CDs is larger than that of g-C3N4, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 transferred to CDs, while the holes remain in the VB of g-C3N4. Besides, the electron transfer became faster due to the excellent electric conductivity and the large specific surface area of CDs. When the CDs/g-C3N4 loaded with the Pt cocatalyst, the electron further transfer from CDs to Pt because Pt owns the larger work function in comparison with CDs. As a result, the lifetime of photogenerated electrons was prolonged and the charge recombination was retarded. There were more electrons reacted with the adsorbed H+ to form H2, while the photogenerated holes in the VB of g-C3N4 were trapped by the sacrificial agent (TEOA). Compared to the pristine g-C3N4, the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites is attributed to the following reasons. (1) CDs/g-C3N4 composites with narrowed bandgap enable to broaden the absorption range of visible light, but it makes little contribution in photocatalytic reaction in our case. (2) The micro-domain heterostructure of CDs/g-C3N4 facilitates the photo-generated carrier transfer and effectively hinder the electron-hole pair recombination. Therefore, more photogenerated electrons migrate to the surface of the catalyst to participate in the H2 production reaction. At the same time, TEOA as a sacrificial agent can consume holes at the VB and enhance the separation of photogenerated carriers. (3) As the CDs increase to certain content, the specific surface area of the catalyst is greatly increased, this can provide more reactive sites for the redox reaction (Figs. S8a, b, c). In the photocatalytic reaction, due to its high conductivity, CDs may act as electron acceptors to promote the dissociation of excitons and improve charge transfer [38,39]. When the CDs reach 0.005%, the instinct mesoporous structure of g-C3N4 was changed by filling CDs into the small pores (less than 5 nm) of g-C3N4 and the relatively larger pores (around 12 nm) were retained (Fig. S8e). Continue increasing CDs may result in the disappearing of small pores and starting to fill the larger pores, which leads to the decrease of surface reaction kinetics and relatively lower hydrogen evolution rate. If keep rising CDs content to 1.0%, the small pores appeared once more, and the small pores were mainly caused by the extra CDs in this condition (Fig. S8f). The maximum hydrogen evolution rate achieved at 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 was attributed to the dramatically increased specific surface area (Fig. S8c), which provides more active sites for the redox reactions. Furthermore, keeping loading CDs to more than 1.0%may lead to a decrease in crystallinity and consequently promote charge recombination, resulting in inferior charge utilization efficiency. Therefore, it is of great significance to manipulate the optimal amount of CDs to achieve the highest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution efficiency.

In summary, the CDs/g-C3N4 composites have been successfully synthesized by a facile strategy with gingko leaves and urea as resources. Under visible light irradiation, there was were two volcano-trends in the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate with the increase of CDs content. The optimal H2 evolution rate of 1.0% CDs/g-C3N4 composites reached 3.12 mmol⋅g−1·h−1, which was 4.1 times higher than that of the pristine g-C3N4. The presence of micro-heterojunction between CDs and g-C3N4 not only promoted the photo-generated charges transfer and reduced their recombination, but also enhanced the visible light absorption. On the other hand, the significantly increased specific surface area caused by the presence of CDs can provide more active-sites for the H2 evolution reaction. Furthermore, DFT theoretical calculations were used to understand the influence of CDs decorated into g-C3N4, which was consistent with the experimental results. This work may provide a new insight into the design and synthesis of high-efficiency and low-cost metal-free photocatalysts, which can be extended to many other fields such as water purification, environmental remediation, energy conversion, and optoelectronic devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The authors would like to appreciate Mr. Jilong Gao from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis. Special thanks for the instrumental or data analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University and Beijing Perfectlight Technology CO, LTD.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xia, B. Cheng, J. Fan, J. Yu, G. Liu, Unraveling Photoexcited Charge Transfer Pathway and Process of CdS/Graphene Nanoribbon Composites toward Visible-Light Photocatalytic Hydrogen Evolution, Small 15(34) (2019) 1902459. https://doi.org/10.1002/smll.201902459.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on quantum dots modified g-C3N4-based photocatalysts with improved photocatalytic activity
Catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chen, X. Bai, A Review on Quantum Dots Modified g-C3N4-Based Photocatalysts with Improved Photocatalytic Activity, Catalysts 10(1) (2020). https://doi.org/10.3390/catal10010142.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ fabrication of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids with enhanced interface connection via calcination of the precursors for photocatalytic H2 evolution
Int J Hydrogen Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Wang, X. Wang, H. Pan, Y. Liu, S. Xu, S. Cao, In situ fabrication of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids with enhanced interface connection via calcination of the precursors for photocatalytic H2 evolution, International Journal of Hydrogen Energy 43(1) (2018) 91-99. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.11.003.
